figures.caption: Fig. 1. From traditional per-chip training from scratch to Therm-FM: finetuning a pretrained PDE foundation model for 3D-IC thermal simulation.

Fig. 2. Workflow of Therm-FM. The left panel shows PDE foundation-model pretraining and lightweight fine-tuning for 3D-IC thermal prediction. The middle panel illustrates the neural-operator backbone with embedding/recovery layers and hierarchical SwinV2 blocks. The right panel presents the two-stage multi-fidelity adaptation process using low- and high-fidelity data.

Fig. 3. Industrial 3D-IC benchmark construction and EMT-based low-fidelity modeling. The detailed package contains heterogeneous core, µbump, TSV, C4 bump, and substrate layers. Core-level power distributions are applied to both the detailed and thermal-equivalent models, where dense TSV and µbump arrays are homogenized into macroscopic equivalent layers for low-cost thermal simulation.
TABLE I GEOMETRIC PARAMETERS OF THE INDUSTRIAL 3D-IC BENCHMARKS.

Fig. 4. Comparison with existing methods on the HS-QC case at 40 × 40 and 64 × 64 resolutions. Each subpanel reports one metric, including RMSE, MAPE, PAPE, Mean error, Max error, and GPU memory. Gray markers denote prior baselines in the legend order, orange denotes SAU-FNO, and blue denotes Therm-FM variants. Lower values are better for all metrics.
TABLE III COMPARISON OF THERM-FM WITH EXISTING ML-BASED THERMAL MODELING METHODS ON HS-OC REFINE1 AND REFINE2
TABLE II COMPARISON OF THERM-FM WITH EXISTING ML-BASED THERMAL MODELING METHODS ON HS-SC REFINE1 AND REFINE2
TABLE IV COMPARISON OF THERM-FM WITH EXISTING ML-BASED THERMAL MODELING METHODS ON IND-8C(UPPER) AND IND-32C(BELOW)
TABLE V TRANSIENT THERMAL PREDICTION RESULTS ON HS-SC, HS-QC, AND HS-OC BENCHMARKS.

Fig. 6. Training-sample sensitivity of Therm-FM on HS-SC, HS-QC, and HS-OC. The gray dashed line denotes the full-data SAU-FNO RMSE baseline. All results are obtained with the Therm-FM-B configuration.
TABLE VI ABLATION ON PRETRAINED INITIALIZATION AND MODEL SCALING.

Fig. 9. Qualitative visualization of transient thermal prediction on two representative cases, HS-SC and HS-OC. Each column corresponds to a sampled time step, and each pair of rows shows the ground-truth and predicted temperature fields for one case.

Fig. 10. Qualitative comparison of steady-state thermal prediction results across five representative cases. For each case, the first row shows the ground-truth temperature field, the second row shows the prediction of Therm-FM, and the third row shows the absolute error map.
